gridually.name: Gridually
gridually.tagline: Spatial memory flashcard app — learn by placing cards on a grid
gridually.method: Spatial memory + SRQ scoring — based on research showing position-based encoding works independently of visual imagery (Bainbridge et al., 2021; Monzel et al., 2024). Combines spaced repetition with spatial encoding — no other flashcard app does both.

pricing.paid_plan: Pro plan for advanced analytics and AI features

features: AI card generation from any source
Import from Anki, Quizlet, CSV, PDF
Calm, focused UI designed for deep study
pros: Unique spatial memory approach — backed by memory palace science
Modern, clean UI that doesn't feel like 2005
AI generates cards from PDFs, notes, or any text
Only app built specifically for people with aphantasia
cons: Smaller community than Anki or Quizlet
Newer app — fewer third-party integrations
Deck library is growing but not as large as Quizlet's 800M+
best_for: Learners who want a modern, visual approach to flashcards
People with aphantasia
Language learners (especially Japanese, Spanish, German)
Students who find Anki too complex or ugly
Anyone who wants AI-powered card creation
gridually.verdict: Best for spatial memory learners who want a modern, effective alternative to traditional flashcard apps.

anki.name: Anki
anki.tagline: The gold standard for spaced repetition — powerful but steep learning curve

pricing.free_details: Completely free on desktop and Android

features: FSRS algorithm for optimal review scheduling
pros: Completely free on desktop and Android
Huge add-on ecosystem for any use case
Full control over card templates and scheduling
cons: Steep learning curve — intimidating for beginners
Dated, ugly UI ('90s desktop aesthetic')
iOS app is $24.99
Setting up add-ons can be frustrating
No built-in AI card generation
Sync can be finicky
best_for: Medical students (USMLE, board exams)
Power users who want full control
Long-term serious learners willing to invest time in setup
Users who want free + open source
anki.verdict: Best for maximum retention if you can tolerate the friction. The gold standard for spaced repetition, but the learning curve and dated UI turn many people away.

quizlet.name: Quizlet
quizlet.tagline: Easiest to start — huge library but increasingly paywalled
quizlet.method: Basic flashcard review with limited spaced repetition

pricing.free_details: Basic flashcards free, many features paywalled

features: Learn mode, Test mode, Match game
pros: 5 minutes to start studying — lowest friction of any app
Good for group study and classrooms
Multiple study modes beyond just flashcards
cons: Weak spaced repetition — not optimized for long-term retention
Increasingly paywalled — free tier keeps shrinking
AI features locked behind paywall
Not ideal for serious, long-term memorization
Quality of shared decks varies wildly
best_for: Beginners who want to start immediately
Short-term study (exam next week)
Group study and classroom use
People who want pre-made content
quizlet.verdict: Great if you want something fast and simple, but not optimized for long-term learning. The paywall keeps growing.

brainscape.name: Brainscape
brainscape.tagline: Confidence-based repetition with a clean UI
cons: Limited free tier — paywalled quickly
Less flexible than Anki
Smaller community and content library
CBR is simpler than true SRS algorithms
best_for: Professional certification prep
Users who want curated, high-quality content
People who prefer a clean, guided experience
brainscape.verdict: A polished option for professional learners, but the limited free tier and simpler algorithm hold it back.

remnote.name: RemNote
remnote.tagline: Notes and flashcards unified in one tool
pros: Notes and flashcards in one place — no duplicate work
Outline format is great for structured learning
cons: Can feel cluttered with too many features
Learning curve for the note system
Mobile apps less polished than desktop
Performance can lag with large notebooks
best_for: Students who take detailed notes AND want flashcards
People who want one tool for everything
Learners who think in outlines
remnote.verdict: Best if you want notes and flashcards tightly connected. The all-in-one approach saves time but can feel overwhelming.

mochi.name: Mochi
mochi.tagline: Markdown-native flashcards for developers and minimalists
pros: Perfect for developers — markdown + code highlighting
cons: Small community
No mobile apps
Limited sharing/collaboration features
Basic UI — minimal by design
best_for: Developers learning programming concepts
Minimalists who want no distractions
Privacy-conscious users (local-first)
mochi.verdict: A developer's flashcard app — lightweight, markdown-native, and no-nonsense. Limited if you need mobile or collaboration.
